cards.title: After AROM, the water stays broken
cards.excerpt: Artificial rupture of membranes means the amniotic sac is opened on purpose. That change is permanent—you cannot reseal it. Labor may pick up, or it may not. Either way, your team will watch infection risk and how long membranes have been open. Ask your OB or midwife what changes for your plan once water is broken.
cards.source_name: The Birth Partner by Penny Simkin
cards.partner_tip: Before anyone breaks the water, ask quietly: once this happens, what path are we on—and can we pause to decide together?

cards.title: Broken water can start a hospital clock
cards.excerpt: Many hospitals track hours from rupture—membranes open—to delivery or next step. Policies vary: some allow many hours if vitals are good; others move toward Pitocin or cesarean sooner. Early labor at the hospital, with little to do, often leads to unnecessary interventions. Know your facility's typical timeline before you're in the room.
cards.partner_tip: Ask the nurse or provider directly: does breaking her water start a clock for us—and what happens at each hour mark?

cards.title: Waiting after AROM can be reasonable
cards.excerpt: If the baby looks well on monitoring and there are no signs of infection, waiting for contractions to strengthen on their own is sometimes appropriate. Use a simple decision framework: what is the problem, what are the options, what are the risks and benefits, and what if we wait? Your clinical team—not this lesson—sets safe limits.
cards.source_name: Evidence Based Birth by Rebecca Dekker, PhD, RN
cards.partner_tip: If someone suggests AROM to speed things up, help her ask: what problem are we fixing, and what happens if we give it more time?

story_segments: Intro: Labor Prep Daily for Sunday, May thirty-first, twenty twenty-six. Today we are looking at breaking the water—also called AROM—and what it means for your timeline.

First idea: once the bag of water is broken on purpose, it stays broken. There is no going back. Your care team will watch for infection and how labor responds. Before anyone proceeds, pause and make sure you both understand the new path.

Second idea: at many hospitals, ruptured membranes start a clock. Policies differ on how many hours are allowed before the next step. Ask clearly whether breaking the water starts a timer for you—and what happens at each mark.

Third idea: waiting is not always wrong. If mother and baby are stable, more time for contractions to build may be safe. Use simple questions: what is the problem, what are our options, and what if we wait? Your OB or midwife decides the limits.
Outro: AROM can shift labor in ways you cannot undo—so ask about the clock, and let your clinical team guide what is safe; this is education, not medical advice.

Policies differ on how many hours are allowed before the next step. Ask clearly whether breaking the water starts a timer for you—and what happens at each mark. Third idea: waiting is not always wrong. If mother and baby are stable, more time for contractions to build may be safe. Use simple questions: what is the problem, what are our options, and what if we wait? Your OB or midwife decides the limits. Outro: AROM can shift labor in ways you cannot undo—so ask about the clock, and let your clinical team guide what is safe; this is education, not medical advice.
